coredata.dc:title: Aggregatable thiol-functionalized carbon dots-based fluorescence strategy for highly sensitive detection of glucose based on target-initiated catalytic oxidation
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Jianling
Yan, Ji
Dou, Baoting
Feng, Qiumei
Miao, Xiangmin
Wang, Po
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have been widely applied in analysis of glucose due to their advantages of ultra-small size and good biocompatibility, but tedious steps and few functional groups associated with current CDs-based biosensors hamper their practical application. Herein, we report the thiol-functionalized CDs (S-CDs) for developing a sensitive and selective glucose biosensor. S-CDs were prepared by a simple hydrothermal carbonization and additional surface modification method, and emitted bright yellow-green light with exceptional pH/salt stability. After exposure to H2O2, S-CDs were transformed into nonluminous S-CDs assembly based on target-initiated catalyzed oxidation of -SH into -S-S-. Given the fact that glucose can be catalyzed decomposition into H2O2 by glucose oxidase, S-CDs were further employed to detect glucose with a low limit of detection (0.03 μM) and high selectivity for discriminating glucose against other saccharine. Finally, S-CDs were applied for glucose sensing in human serum with satisfactory result. This is the first documented example of S-CDs for glucose sensing. We expected this work will open new avenues for developing high-performance fluorescence biosensors based on CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thiol-functionalized CDs
Glucose
H2O2
Target-initiated catalyzed oxidation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of S-CDs to H2O2
Fluorescence response of S-CDs to glucose
Selectivity of S-CDs-based biosensor for glucose assay

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustration of preparation of CDs and S-CDs. (B) TEM image of S-CDs in PBS. (C) FT-IR spectra of CDs, MPA and S-CDs. (D) EDS spectra of CDs and S-CDs. (E) UV–vis absorption spectra of S-CDs, and the inset shows the visible (left) and fluorescent (right) images of S-CDs under 365 nm excitation. (F) Normalized excitation and emission wavelengths of S-CDs. (G) Fluorescence spectra of S-CDs under the excitation of different wavelengths from 345 to 455 nm.
(A) Working principle for H2O2 sensing based on S-CDs. (B) Emission spectra of S-CDs under different conditions. (C) TEM image of S-CDs in the presence of 500 μM H2O2.

(A) Emission spectra of S-CDs corresponding to H2O2 with different concentrations. (B) Working line between F0/F and CH2O2 in the range of 0.01-5.0 μM. The error bars represent the standard deviations of five repeated measurements.

(A) Working principle for glucose sensing based on target-initiated the fluorescence quenching of S-CDs. (B) Emission spectra of S-CDs in the presence of glucose at increasing dosages. (B) Working line between F0/F vs Cglu in the range of 0.1-10 μM. The error bars represent the standard deviations of five repeated measurements.

(A) Fluorescence intensity of the sensing system containing different substances in the presence of I− and GOx: (a) S-CDs, (b) S-CDs + glucose, (c) S-CDs + NaHCO3, (d) S-CDs + NaNO3, (e) S-CDs + L-cys, (f) S-CDs + Try, (g) S-CDs + Iso, (h) S-CDs + GSH, (i) S-CDs + UA, (j) S-CDs + glucose + NaHCO3, (k) S-CDs + glucose + NaNO3, (l) S-CDs + glucose + Try, (m) S-CDs + glucose + Iso, (n) S-CDs + glucose + UA, (o) S-CDs + glucose + L-cys, (p) S-CDs + glucose + GSH, (q) S-CDs + glucose +90 °C/5.0 min-treated l-cys, and (r) S-CDs + glucose +90 °C/5.0 min-treated GSH. (B) Fluorescence intensity of the sensing system containing different substances in the presence of I− and GOx: (a) S-CDs, (b) S-CDs + glucose, (c) S-CDs + Mal, (d) S-CDs + Lac, (e) S-CDs + Suc, (f) S-CDs + Fru, (g) S-CDs + Xyl, (h) S-CDs + Man, (i) S-CDs + glucose + Mal, (j) S-CDs + glucose + Lac, (k) S-CDs + glucose + Suc, (l) S-CDs + glucose + Fru, (m) S-CDs + glucose + Xyl, (n) S-CDs + glucose + Man. The concentrations of all analytes are 1000 μM. (C) Emission spectra of sensing system upon the addition of diluted serum (a), diluted serum + 5.0 μM glucose (b), and 10.0 μM glucose (c). The error bars represent the standard deviations of five repeated measurements.

head.ce:title: Aggregatable thiol-functionalized carbon dots-based fluorescence strategy for highly sensitive detection of glucose based on target-initiated catalytic oxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Jiangsu Normal University, Xuzhou, 221116, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Jiangsu Normal University, Xuzhou 221116, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, Sun Yat-Sen University, Guangzhou, 510275, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry, Sun Yat-Sen University, Guangzhou 510275, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been widely applied in analysis of glucose due to their advantages of ultra-small size and good biocompatibility, but tedious steps and few functional groups associated with current CDs-based biosensors hamper their practical application. Herein, we report the thiol-functionalized CDs (S-CDs) for developing a sensitive and selective glucose biosensor. S-CDs were prepared by a simple hydrothermal carbonization and additional surface modification method, and emitted bright yellow-green light with exceptional pH/salt stability. After exposure to H2O2, S-CDs were transformed into nonluminous S-CDs assembly based on target-initiated catalyzed oxidation of -SH into -S-S-. Given the fact that glucose can be catalyzed decomposition into H2O2 by glucose oxidase, S-CDs were further employed to detect glucose with a low limit of detection (0.03 μM) and high selectivity for discriminating glucose against other saccharine. Finally, S-CDs were applied for glucose sensing in human serum with satisfactory result. This is the first documented example of S-CDs for glucose sensing. We expected this work will open new avenues for developing high-performance fluorescence biosensors based on CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetes, the third largest noninfectious disease, occurs either when pancreas does not generate sufficient insulin or when body can not employ insulin to regulate the blood glucose, and thus causes serious damage to nervous systems and blood vessels [1,2]. Hence, early and accurate diagnosis of diabetes is of significant importance to assure human fitness. Many investigations demonstrated that glucose level in biological liquids is relevant to diabetes [3,4], and thus abundant glucose biosensors have been invented [5–8]. Except for high performance liquid chromatography and ion chromatography, most biosensors were developed on electrochemical, colorimetric and fluorometric techniques [9–11]. Electrochemical biosensors require the complicated immobilization of biomolecules/enzymes; colorimetric ones suffered from low sensitivity [12–14]. Fluorometric biosensors not only simplify the steps, but also abate the background noise and heighten the sensitivity, and thus are more suitable in quantifying glucose [15]. For a biosensor, it is crucial to prepare a signal element with best properties. Good water solubility/dispersion is beneficial to avoid the use of organic solvent; high quantum yield is favorable to reduce the dosage [16,17]. Unfortunately, organic dyes suffer from harsh preparation conditions, severe aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect and poor water solubility/dispersion [18,19]; quantum dots are limited to high toxicity [20,21].

Up to now, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted considerable interest because of small size, low cost, good biosafety, and tunable emission [22–27]. These characteristics recommend them for widespread application in analysis of gene, microRNA, metal ions and glucose [28–36]. The strategies are basically divided into three aspects. The first is to introduce AgNPs, and is capable of probing glucose based on AgNPs’ quenching on CDs’ fluorescence and H2O2-initiated erosion of AgNPs into Ag+ [34]. Unfortunately, they are limited to complicated steps and weak stability. The second is to prepare pH-response CDs, which detect glucose based on variation in pH caused by glucoinc acid (GA) deriving from catalyzing hydrolysis of glucose [35], however resulting in low sensitivity because of the requirement of abundant GA. The third one is to prepare boronic acid-functionalized CDs, which detect glucose through covalent binding between cis-diols and boronic acid [36], but easily causing false diagnosis due to the presence of biomolecules with cis-diols group. Furthermore, most CDs emit fluorescence with maximum wavelength < 500 nm, suffer from small Stokes shift, and only have −COOH or –NH2 or –B(OH)2 group [31,37–39]. Therefore, it is highly urgent to prepare CDs with long emission wavelength and functional group, and to propose an ingenious strategy for glucose assay, but still remains a challenge.

Herein, a novel kind of CDs with thiol group and long emission wavelength (S-CDs) were prepared via a hydrothermal carbonization and additional surface modification method. Optical measurements showed that S-CDs displayed bright fluorescence emission at 525 nm with high stability and excellent water dispersion. Compared with CDs, S-CDs displayed high response to H2O2 based on target-triggered formation of -S-S- and ACQ effect of S-CDs. This property enables S-CDs to sensitively and selectively probe glucose based on glucose oxidase (GOx)-induced catalytic oxidation of glucose into H2O2. We anticipate the S-CDs will find more application in analysis of glucose/H2O2-related diseases, and the present study will supply more data for devising CDs with best properties for developing fluorescence biosensors.

0.45 g Malic acid was added into 10 mL of water, and the solution was heated to 200 °C for 5.0 h. After cooling down, the precipitates were removed through centrifugation (5000 rpm, 5.0 min), and the supernate was dialyzed for 48 h (molecular interception = 500) to get CDs.

19 mg 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and 22 mg N-hydroxy succinimide (NHS) were added into 10 mL of CDs solution (0.1 mg/mL) and incubated for 0.5 h. 4.4 mg mercaptopropylamine (MPA) was subsequently added. After 4.0 h, S-CDs were acquired through dialyzing for 48 h (molecular interception = 500) to remove un-reacted raw materials.
The H2O2 assay was conducted in a 100 μL of 0.1 M phosphate buffer saline (PBS) (0.1 M NaCl, pH = 7) containing 10 μg/mL S-CDs, 30 μM NaI, and H2O2 with different amounts for 30 min.
The glucose assay was conducted in a 100 μL of 0.1 M PBS (0.1 M NaCl, pH = 7) containing 10 μg/mL S-CDs, 30 μM NaI, 60 μg/mL GOx, and glucose with different amounts for 40 min.

S-CDs were prepared by a two-step strategy: hydrothermal carbonization and covalent interaction, and the schematic illustration was manifested in Fig. 1 A. Malic acid was chosen as raw material because of high-coke content and −COOH group, permitting the fabrication of bright CDs with −COOH group. To acquire CDs with best properties, the hydrothermal carbonization conditions were optimized to be 200 °C for 5.0 h. The as-fabricated CDs covalently reacted with MPA to produce S-CDs. To confirm the preparation of S-CDs, TEM and FT-IR measurements were successively conducted. It revealed from Fig. 1B that S-CDs are well-dispersed with a diameter at 4.0 nm. In Fig. 1C, there are three FT-IR peaks at 3442, 1647, and 1400 cm−1 for CDs, which are respectively assigned to −OH, –C = O and –C–O– groups, implying the presence of −COOH. In contrast, S-CDs showed new FT-IR peaks at 2494, 1593 and 1490 cm−1, which are respectively attributable to –S–H, –N–H, and –C–N groups in MPA, justifying its linkage with CDs. The same conclusion can also be obtained from EDS, 1H NMR spectra and XPS spectra (Fig. 1D, Fig. S1 and S2). Only two peaks at 0.3 keV and 0.55 keV corresponding to C and O elements were detected for CDs; in sharp contrast, S-CDs demonstrated two additional peaks at 0.4 keV and 2.3 keV from N and S elements. In 1H NMR spectra, the peaks in the range of 2.5–3.5 ppm assigning to −CH2– were found for S-CDs. In addition, new peaks corresponding to N and S elements in XPS curve of S-CDs appeared compared with that of CDs. More importantly, for N 1s, the XPS peak was recorded at 400.3 eV, which was attributable to the amide nitrogen [40].

Fig. 1E presented the UV–vis spectrum of S-CDs, in which only one absorption peak (280 nm) was recorded and there was no absorption from 400 to 500 nm due to the ultra-small size, thus displaying a high-transparency visible image. Meanwhile, a bright emission was observed under the excitation of 365 nm (inset in Fig. 1E). Under 405 nm excitation, fluorescence emission at 525 nm was observed, and thus the Stokes shift was calculated to be 120 nm (Fig. 1F), efficiently minimizing the background interference. Besides, the lifetime was measured to be 4.54 ns (Fig. S2A and Table S1). With the excitation wavelength raising from 345 to 455 nm, the fluorescence peak shifted from 476 to 556 nm, and the emission intensity initially raised and finally declined after 405 nm, implying the excitation wavelength-dependent behavior (Fig. 1G). Further, optical stability was researched through adding S-CDs in different-pH and high-concentration NaCl solutions. As pH value increased from 3.0 to 8.0, the emission intensity of S-CDs slightly varied; with pH value further raised, fluorescence gradually declined (Fig. S3A), validating the strong stability. Increasing NaCl concentration from 0 to 9.0 mM and storing time from 0 to 24 h also caused little influence on fluorescence signal (Fig. S3B and 3C). Furthermore, five differently prepared S-CDs demonstrated the similar fluorescence peak and intensity (Fig. S3D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of S-CDs to H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: As depicted in Fig. 2 A, individual S-CDs exhibited a bright yellow-green emission, whereas significantly quenched fluorescence was observed upon the addition of H2O2 by using I− as co-reactant. This decreased fluorescence was ascribed to the formation of -S-S- bonds and subsequently initiated aggregation of S-CDs, as well as aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching. Control experiments in Fig. 2B demonstrated that I- or H2O2 can not quench the fluorescence emission of S-CDs. However, the fluorescence intensity declined from 1426 a.u. to 679 a.u. upon the addition of 50 μM H2O2 in the presence of I-. As H2O2 concentration increased to 500 μM, approximately 75 %-quenched fluorescence emission was observed. Accordingly, the lifetime was tested to be 5.60 ns (Fig. S1B and Table S1), which is about the same as that of S-CDs, indicating the static quenching of S-CDs by H2O2. S-CDs were proven to be non-emissive in aggregated state (Fig. S4). In addition, many works have verified that H2O2 can catalyze oxidation of –SH into -S-S- [41]. Thus, it was deduced that the quenched fluorescence is ascribed to H2O2-initiated oxidative aggregation through the formation of -S-S- bond. TEM technique was employed to confirm this speculation (Fig. 2C). The morphology transformed from 4.0 nm-dot to 110 nm-sphere, implying multiple S-CDs aggregated together into larger-diameter nanospheres. The sharp fluorescence decrease could also be supported by difference in UV–vis characterizations (Fig. S5). Negligible absorption peak in the range of 200−260 nm for S-CDs was found, whereas one absorption peak at 225 nm for S-CDs/H2O2 appeared, and the solution color changed from colorless into yellow. In contrast, CDs displayed very weak response to H2O2 (Fig. S6), justifying the great role of –SH in detection of H2O2 and judging the successful linkage of MPA too.

Under the optimized conditions (Fig. S7), we challenged S-CDs’ detection capability by adding H2O2 with differing concentrations into reaction solution. As H2O2 amount raised, the fluorescence emission of S-CDs underwent a noticeable decrease, presenting an opposite relationship (Fig. 3 A). This is reasonable that more H2O2 catalyzed more S-CDs oxidative aggregation, thus higher-efficiency quenching the fluorescence emission. To quantitatively assess the response of S-CDs to H2O2, calibration curve was plotted by using F0/F (F and F0 are the emission intensity of S-CDs in the presence/absence of H2O2, respectively) and H2O2 concentration (CH2O2) as ordinate and abscissa, respectively. As seen in Fig. 3B, F0/F was linearly proportional to CH2O2 within a range from 0.01 to 10 μM. The resultant equation was expressed as F0/F = 1.0225 + 0.0245 CH2O2 with a co-efficient of 0.9987. The limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 0.003 μM based on 3σ. As far as we are concerned, H2O2 is a metabolite from glucose catalyzed oxidation by GOx. Hence, the unique response of S-CDs to H2O2 enabled them possible to sensitively probe glucose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of S-CDs to glucose
ce:section.ce:para: The working principle for glucose probing based on S-CDs is manifested in Fig. 4 A. Upon the addition of glucose, GOx catalyzed it into H2O2 [42], which catalyzed oxidation of S-CDs into aggresome. Consequently, the fluorescence emission of S-CDs was switched to the “off” state. Since the quenching fluorescence of S-CDs was directly related to the amount of H2O2, which was subsequently dependent on the concentration of glucose, glucose assay can be readily acquired.

Sensor detection capability of S-CDs for glucose was evaluated under the optimized conditions (Fig. S8). A series of testing samples containing glucose in the range of 0.1–1000 μM were separately added into the reaction solution. As glucose dosage raised, S-CDs’ quenching fluorescence aggravated, exhibiting a similar negative relevance with that of H2O2 (Fig. 4B). Furthermore, there is a high linear relationship (R2 = 0.9836) between F0/F (F and F0 are the emission intensity of S-CDs in the presence/absence of glucose, respectively) and glucose dosage (Cglu) in the range of 0.1−50 μM (Fig. 4C). The regression equation was F0/F = 1.003 + 0.00809 Cglu with a 0.03 μM LOD and a 0.10 μM limit of quantification (LOQ). Noticeably, the LOD was lower than or comparable to that of AgNPs/pH/boronic acid-based fluorescence biosensors (Table S2). This high sensitivity might be from S-CDs’ abundant –SH groups, permitting for a significant increase in agglomeration degree of S-CDs in the presence of only a small bit of target object. Besides, this low LOD is of great beneficial to practical application because it allowed the testing sample to be sufficiently diluted to avoid the interference from serum or urine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of S-CDs-based biosensor for glucose assay
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity is an important parameter in appraising the sensing performance of a biosensor. With this in mind, we first research the response of S-CDs to potential interferents, which might co-exist in serum or urine with glucose. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence intensity is approximately 3.00, 3.05, 3.18, 3.36, 2.97, 3.06, and 3.12 times higher than that of glucose for NaHCO3, NaNO3, l-cysteine (L-cys), tryptophan (Try), isoleucine (Iso), glutathione (GSH), and uric acid (UA), respectively, indicating that they have very little affect on quenching fluorescence emission of S-CDs. Further, we mixed these interferents with glucose to investigate the anti-interference ability of S-CDs in analysis of glucose. NaHCO3, NaNO3, Try, Iso and UA caused little affect on S-CDs in response to glucose; whereas, adding l-cys or GSH prevented glucose from decreasing the fluorescence emission. This might be ascribed to the fact that l-cys/GSH can reduce S-CDs aggresomes into S-CDs to recover the fluorescence emission. In this context, a thermal treatment method was introduced to overcome the interference of l-cys/GSH. It was noted that 90 °C/5.0 min-treatment efficiently overcame the affect of l-cys/GSH in analysis of glucose.

Glucose analogues including maltose (Mal), lactose (Lac), sucrose (Suc), fructose (Fru), xylose (Xyl) and mannose (Man) were employed to study the specific response of S-CDs to glucose. As illustrated in Fig. 5B, these six analogues caused negligible effect on quenching fluorescence of S-CDs. Furthermore, the co-existence of one of the analogues with glucose hardly interfere with fluorescence response of S-CDs toward glucose. Taken together, S-CDs has the capability of differencing glucose from other saccharides and determining glucose in complex system with strong anti-interference ability. The selective response of S-CDs to glucose was based on H2O2-initiated oxidative aggregation and specific catalytic oxidation of glucose into H2O2 by GOx. Unlike glucose, other substances can not be recognized by GOx; thus, H2O2 can not be produced, resulting in little effect on fluorescence quenching.

For a biosensor, the ultimate aim is to be applied in realistic sample. To challenge the practical application performance, we first employed S-CDs to detect glucose in a commercial serum (the expression level in which can not be determined by S-CDs) by a spiking method. As depicted in Table S3, for 3, 20, and 40 μM glucose sensing, the recoveries of 96.7 %, 98.1 % and 102.7 % and relative standard deviations (RSDs) of 3.63 %, 1.48 %, and 2.56 % were acquired. These acceptable recoveries and low RSDs demonstrated the high accuracy and good precision of S-CDs in analysis of glucose. Further, we employ S-CDs to detect glucose in human serum from a healthy adult on an empty stomach, and the measured value was compared with a blood glucose monitor. First, glucose level was recorded to be 5.1 mM by blood glucose monitor. The serum was acquired from the blood through centrifugation at 2500 rpm for 3.0 min. Owing to the high sensitivity of S-CDs for glucose, the human serum was allowed to be 1000-fold diluted. Noticeably, addition of diluted serum sample caused the fluorescence emission of S-CDs quenching (Fig. 5C). With 5.0 μM and 10.0 μM glucose further being separately added, the fluorescence emission gradually declined too. According to working curve, the expression level of glucose in human serum is 5.3 mM, which was approximately identical with that measured by blood glucose monitor and corresponded well to glucose level in serum of healthy persons.

In summary, a novel kind of bright yellow-green light-emitting S-CDs were triumphantly prepared via a simple hydrothermal carbonization and additional surface modification method. S-CDs possessed strong pH/salt stability and displayed excellent response to H2O2. The quenching behavior can be ascribed to H2O2-triggered oxidative aggregation of S-CDs and S-CDs’ ACQ effect. This unique property enabled S-CDs to sensitively and selectively detect glucose based on GOx-initiated catalytic oxidation of glucose into H2O2. Furthermore, S-CDs were applied in analysis of glucose in serum with acceptable result. Compared with previously reported biosensors, our biosensor avoids the use of AgNPs, proposes a novel strategy for glucose assay, possesses high sensitivity and good selectivity, and thus has potential application in diagnosis of H2O2 and glucose-related diseases. Moreover, this strategy was also applicable to other materials with thiol groups, thus playing a significant part in developing high-performance biosensors.

Jianling Chen: Conceptualization, Project administration, Writing - original draft. Ji Yan: Conceptualization, Project administration, Writing - original draft. Baoting Dou: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Qiumei Feng: Validation, Data curation. Xiangmin Miao: Methodology, Formal analysis. Po Wang: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BE2019645), the Natural Science Foundation of Xuzhou City (KC19161), the PAPD, the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX20_2351), and the Six Talent Peak Project for distinguished scholars in Jiangsu Province (2019-SWYY-084).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.129325.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jianling Chen is currently a doctoral student of Sun Yat-Sen University and Jiangsu Normal University majoring in Analytical Chemistry.
Ji Yan is now a graduate student in School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Jiangsu Normal University. His research interests mainly focus on biosensors and analytical chemistry.

Baoting Dou is currently a lecturer of Jiangsu Normal University. She received her Ph.D. degree in Analytical Chemistry from Southwest University in 2019. Her research interests mainly focus on the construction of biosensors.

Qiumei Feng obtained her Ph.D. degree in Analytical Chemistry from Nanjing University in 2016. Currently, she is an associate professor of Jiangsu Normal University. Her research interests mainly include analytical chemistry, biosensors, and the synthesis and applications of nanomaterials.

Xiangmin Miao obtained her Ph.D. degree in Analytical Chemistry from Sun Yat-Sen University, China, in 2012. Now, she is working in Jiangsu Normal University and the current fields of interest were mainly biosensors, optical and electrochemical nano-biological analysis and immunoassay.

Po Wang obtained his Ph.D. degree in Analytical Chemistry from Sun Yat-Sen University, China, in 2011. Then, he worked as an associate professor in Jiangsu Normal University. His scientific interests mainly include analytical chemistry, biosensors, and the synthesis and applications of nanomaterials.
